title: Whether municipal markets should resist becoming food courts
summary: Argue whether traditional municipal markets should be standardised into glossy food halls, or whether their mess and unevenness is the point.
topic: Whether municipal markets should resist the pressure to become tidy food courts
prompt: Write a 350–500-word argumentative essay about whether traditional municipal markets — Mercado de Abastos in Oaxaca, Borough Market in London, the markets of Penang or Naples — should resist the pressure to be redesigned into clean, standardised food halls aimed at tourists and lunchtime office workers. Use a 'what' cleft, a concession ('Granted, …' or 'Admittedly, …'), at least one passive construction, and one participle clause. Anchor the argument in at least one specific market or city. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: What a refurbished food hall quietly removes from a market is something its consultants have not been hired to…
Granted, the older municipal market in its un-refurbished form does carry real problems — drainage, refrigeration, accessibility — that cannot…
Walk into the Mercado de Abastos in Oaxaca on a Tuesday morning and the first thing you notice is that it is not designed for…

hintVocab.phrase: to be priced out of one's stall
hintVocab.meaningVi: bị đẩy ra khỏi quầy hàng do giá thuê cao

thiết kế lại hướng đến du khách

hintVocab.phrase: to maintain a stall across generations
hintVocab.meaningVi: duy trì một quầy hàng qua các thế hệ

hintVocab.phrase: to be flattened into a brand

quầy hàng dễ chụp ảnh đẹp đăng mạng
cung cấp nhu yếu phẩm cho khu phố
chợ có sinh hoạt thực sự

sampleText:
Walk into the Mercado de Abastos in Oaxaca on a Tuesday morning, and the first thing you notice is that it is not designed for tourists. The lighting is fluorescent. The aisles are crowded. The fishmonger is shouting prices to the woman two stalls down. Someone is gutting a chicken at a steady, indifferent pace. The smell is not unpleasant; it is simply unmistakable. To a visitor used to glass-fronted food halls, the experience can feel disorganised. To anyone shopping for a household of six, it is the most efficient room in the city.

What a refurbished food hall quietly removes from a market is something its consultants have not been hired to measure. Long-time stallholders, who cannot meet the higher rent of a renovated unit and whose customers do not visit the new courtyard café, are quietly displaced. Their replacements — three identical artisanal sandwich counters, a craft beer bar, a stall selling reclaimed wood — produce a building that photographs well, generates higher rents for the operator, and bears progressively less resemblance to a market.

Granted, the older municipal market in its un-refurbished form does carry real problems that cannot be wished away. Drainage backs up. Refrigeration is uneven. Accessibility for older shoppers, people with prams, or anyone using a wheelchair is, in many of these buildings, frankly poor. The case for serious investment in the physical fabric of the market is not the case against the market. A building can be cleaner, safer, and better lit without losing what made it a market in the first place.

Even so, the kind of refurbishment that municipal authorities increasingly favour does not stop at drainage. It rezones, raises rents, imposes uniform tenancy templates, and pushes out the long-time traders on whom the market's actual customer base depends. What is being sold to the city as 'modernisation' is, in practice, a quieter form of tenant clearance. The trader who has sold dried chillies from the same stall for thirty-two years cannot afford the new rent. Her customers — who came for the chillies, not the building — eventually stop coming as well.

Admittedly, some markets have managed renovation without losing their character. Several Spanish municipalities have funded structural upgrades while keeping rent ceilings and tenancy protections that genuinely protect incumbents. These are not romantic experiments. They are evidence that the choice between a working market and a clean building is, in many cases, false.

What needs defending here is not nostalgia for dirt. It is the lived-in market — the building where neighbourhood provisioning, intergenerational stallholding, and a particular form of working-class commerce can still take place — and the recognition that this kind of room, once flattened into a brand, does not return.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to name a hidden cost of refurbishment
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What a refurbished food hall quietly removes from a market is something its consultants have not been hired to measure' dùng cấu trúc 'What … is …' để chỉ ra một mất mát mà các quy trình đo lường chính thức bỏ qua. Trạng từ 'quietly' và mệnh đề chèn 'its consultants have not been hired to measure' tạo nên một sự đối chiếu sắc bén.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What a refurbished food hall quietly removes from a market is something its consultants have not been hired to measure' uses a what-cleft to name a loss that formal measurement processes overlook. The adverb 'quietly' and the embedded clause 'its consultants have not been hired to measure' build a pointed contrast.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Continuous passive within a pseudo-cleft for political diagnosis
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What is being sold to the city as modernisation is, in practice, a quieter form of tenant clearance' kết hợp pseudo-cleft ('What is … is …') với dạng bị động tiếp diễn ('is being sold'). Cấu trúc này cho phép tác giả tách bạch một thuật ngữ chính trị ('modernisation') khỏi thực chất bên dưới nó ('tenant clearance') trong một câu duy nhất.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What is being sold to the city as modernisation is, in practice, a quieter form of tenant clearance' combines a pseudo-cleft ('What is … is …') with the continuous passive ('is being sold'). The structure lets the writer separate a political euphemism ('modernisation') from its underlying reality ('tenant clearance') in a single sentence.

sampleTranslationVi:
Đi vào Mercado de Abastos ở Oaxaca vào một sáng thứ Ba, điều đầu tiên bạn nhận thấy là nó không được thiết kế cho du khách. Đèn là đèn huỳnh quang. Các lối đi đông đúc. Người bán cá đang la giá với người phụ nữ cách hai quầy. Có ai đó đang mổ một con gà với tốc độ ổn định, dửng dưng. Mùi không phải là khó chịu; nó chỉ đơn giản là không thể nhầm lẫn được. Đối với một du khách quen với các khu ẩm thực mặt kính, trải nghiệm có thể cảm thấy lộn xộn. Đối với bất kỳ ai đi chợ cho một hộ gia đình sáu người, đó là căn phòng hiệu quả nhất trong thành phố.

Điều mà một khu ẩm thực được cải tạo lặng lẽ loại bỏ khỏi một chợ là điều mà các cố vấn của nó không được thuê để đo lường. Những người bán hàng lâu năm, không thể đáp ứng mức thuê cao hơn của một đơn vị được cải tạo và khách hàng của họ không ghé quán cà phê mới trong sân, bị buộc phải dời đi một cách lặng lẽ. Người thay thế họ — ba quầy bánh sandwich thủ công giống hệt nhau, một quán bia thủ công, một quầy bán gỗ tái chế — tạo ra một tòa nhà chụp ảnh đẹp, tạo ra mức thuê cao hơn cho người vận hành, và càng ngày càng giống chợ ít hơn.

Thừa nhận rằng, chợ đô thị cũ ở dạng chưa được cải tạo có những vấn đề thực sự không thể mong muốn cho biến mất. Hệ thống thoát nước bị nghẽn. Việc làm lạnh không đồng đều. Khả năng tiếp cận cho người mua sắm lớn tuổi, người có xe đẩy, hay bất kỳ ai dùng xe lăn, trong nhiều tòa nhà này, thực sự là kém. Lập luận cho việc đầu tư nghiêm túc vào cấu trúc vật lý của chợ không phải là lập luận chống lại chợ. Một tòa nhà có thể sạch hơn, an toàn hơn, và được chiếu sáng tốt hơn mà không mất đi điều đã làm cho nó trở thành một chợ ngay từ đầu.

Dẫu vậy, loại cải tạo mà chính quyền thành phố ngày càng ưa chuộng không dừng lại ở việc thoát nước. Nó phân lại khu vực, tăng tiền thuê, áp đặt các mẫu hợp đồng thuê đồng nhất, và đẩy đi những người buôn bán lâu năm mà cơ sở khách hàng thực sự của chợ phụ thuộc vào. Điều đang được bán cho thành phố như 'hiện đại hóa', trong thực tế, là một hình thức giải tỏa người thuê yên lặng hơn. Người bán hàng đã bán ớt khô từ cùng một quầy trong ba mươi hai năm không thể trả nổi mức thuê mới. Khách hàng của bà — những người đến vì ớt, không phải vì tòa nhà — cuối cùng cũng dừng đến.

Thừa nhận rằng, một số chợ đã quản lý việc cải tạo mà không mất đi tính cách của mình. Một số đô thị Tây Ban Nha đã tài trợ các nâng cấp kết cấu trong khi vẫn duy trì trần thuê và các bảo vệ tài sản thuê thực sự bảo vệ người đang sử dụng. Đây không phải là những thí nghiệm lãng mạn. Chúng là bằng chứng rằng sự lựa chọn giữa một chợ vận hành và một tòa nhà sạch sẽ, trong nhiều trường hợp, là sai lầm.

Điều cần được bảo vệ ở đây không phải là sự hoài niệm về sự bẩn thỉu. Đó là chợ có sinh hoạt thực sự — tòa nhà nơi cung cấp nhu yếu phẩm cho khu phố, việc kinh doanh quầy hàng liên thế hệ, và một hình thức thương mại lao động đặc biệt vẫn có thể diễn ra — và sự công nhận rằng loại căn phòng này, một khi đã bị làm phẳng thành một thương hiệu, không quay trở lại.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to name a hidden cost of refurbishment?
options: Hired to measure consultants its what a refurbished food hall removes is something not have been.
What a refurbished food hall quietly removes from a market is something its consultants have not been hired to measure.
From a market quietly removes a refurbished food hall what is something consultants its not hired to measure have been.
Have been hired to measure not its consultants what a refurbished food hall is something quietly removes from a market.
mcQuestions.explanation: The cleft 'What … is …' delays the writer's evaluation ('something its consultants have not been hired to measure') to the emphatic end position.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What … is …' with the writer's diagnosis placed after 'is'.

options: A tenant who refuses to leave a building under any circumstances
A large, prominent tenant whose presence in a market or shopping centre attracts customers and supports smaller tenants
A tenant who lives close to a port
A landlord who owns multiple buildings
mcQuestions.explanation: An anchor tenant is a big, recognisable tenant — a major fishmonger, a famous butcher, a celebrated restaurant — whose presence draws people to a market and helps sustain the smaller stalls around them.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'anchor' as 'holding things in place' — what kind of tenant would do that for a market?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses 'granted' correctly as a concession?
options: Granted, the older municipal market in its un-refurbished form does carry real problems that cannot be wished away.
The older municipal market granted real problems in its un-refurbished form cannot be wished away that does carry.
In its un-refurbished form granted the older municipal market wished away cannot be that does carry real problems.
Real problems granted the older municipal market in its un-refurbished form that cannot be wished away does carry.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted,' followed by a complete clause grants a real point to the opposing argument before the essay continues.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option places 'Granted,' before a full, grammatical sentence?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a continuous passive within a pseudo-cleft to expose a euphemism?
options: What is being sold to the city as 'modernisation' is, in practice, a quieter form of tenant clearance.
Modernisation the city sold what is being to as is in practice tenant clearance a quieter form.
A quieter form of tenant clearance what is being sold to the city as modernisation in practice is.
In practice the city as modernisation sold what is being to is a quieter form of tenant clearance.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'is being sold' is the continuous passive inside a pseudo-cleft ('What is … is …'), which separates the marketed term from the underlying reality.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for 'What is being sold … is …' as a single combined structure.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to displace incumbent traders' mean?
options: To assign incumbent traders to new electronic payment systems
To force the existing, established stallholders to leave their stalls or the market entirely
To formally retire incumbent traders at the end of their careers
To physically move incumbent traders to a different floor of the same building
mcQuestions.explanation: An incumbent trader is the existing tenant; displacement here is the indirect eviction caused by rent rises and tenancy template changes rather than formal eviction.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Incumbent' means currently holding the position; 'displace' means push out — apply both to traders.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option best paraphrases 'progressively less resemblance to a market'?
options: Each year the building looks more and more like a real market.
Over time the building looks less and less like a market and more like something else (a brand, a food hall, a courtyard café).
A market that progresses to other functions.
A building whose appearance changes randomly from year to year.
mcQuestions.explanation: The phrase 'progressively less' marks a continuing, gradual transformation: the building accumulates features (sandwich counters, beer bars) that drift it further from being a market.
mcQuestions.hint: What direction does 'progressively less' move in — toward or away from looking like a market?

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'lived-in market' mean in this essay?
options: A market where the stallholders sleep on the premises
A market that is part of the actual daily life of residents — not a curated experience for visitors
A market that has been certified as continuously occupied for fifty years
A market converted into residential housing
mcQuestions.explanation: A 'lived-in market' is one that is embedded in residents' routine provisioning, not redesigned around tourists or office lunches.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'lived-in' as 'genuinely used in daily life' — what kind of market would that be?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a non-defining relative clause to add context about long-time stallholders?
options: Long-time stallholders, who cannot meet the higher rent of a renovated unit and whose customers do not visit the new courtyard café, are quietly displaced.
Who cannot meet the higher rent long-time stallholders and whose customers do not visit the new courtyard café are quietly displaced.
Are quietly displaced long-time stallholders who cannot meet the higher rent and whose customers do not visit the new courtyard café.
Whose customers do not visit the new courtyard café long-time stallholders who cannot meet the higher rent of a renovated unit are quietly displaced.
mcQuestions.explanation: The clauses 'who cannot meet the higher rent of a renovated unit and whose customers do not visit the new courtyard café' are non-defining (set off by commas), adding extra information about all the long-time stallholders, not restricting which ones are meant.
mcQuestions.hint: Note the commas: non-defining relative clauses are set off by commas on both sides.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a return to the main argument after a concession?
options: For example,
Even so, the kind of refurbishment that municipal authorities increasingly favour does not stop at drainage.
On the contrary, drainage is always best.
Furthermore, all renovation is harmless.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Even so' grants the concession (real building problems exist) and pivots to the main argument that what passes for renovation goes far beyond fixing them.
mcQuestions.hint: Which phrase both accepts the concession and reasserts the writer's position?

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's central position?
options: All municipal markets should remain exactly as they are forever.
All municipal markets should be modernised into clean food halls immediately.
Municipal markets should be physically upgraded where genuinely needed, but the working market — incumbents, rent protections, neighbourhood customers — must be deliberately preserved.
Tourists should be banned from municipal markets.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay supports targeted infrastructure investment but rejects the rezoning, rent rises, and tenancy template changes that effectively clear out incumbents.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraphs three and five for the writer's specific qualifications.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence maintains the most formal academic register?
options: Honestly food halls are basically just shopping centres pretending to be markets.
Old markets are way cooler than new ones, full stop, no question.

Refurbishment programmes that combine higher rents with uniform tenancy templates effectively transfer market space from incumbent traders to higher-rent operators while presenting the change as modernisation.
It's kind of obvious that fancy food courts are killing real markets and we should stop them.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses formal vocabulary ('refurbishment programmes', 'tenancy templates', 'incumbent traders', 'higher-rent operators') and avoids contractions, slang, and informal intensifiers.

options: An economy run primarily by miniature toy traders
An economic system in which a large number of small, independent traders make up a significant share of trade
An economy specifically for trading small items
A national economy below a certain GDP threshold
mcQuestions.explanation: A small-trader economy is one where many independent stallholders, shopkeepers, and informal sellers — rather than large corporate retailers — handle a meaningful share of commerce.
mcQuestions.hint: What kind of economy depends on many small, independent traders rather than a few large chains?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a defining relative clause?
options: The trader who has sold dried chillies from the same stall for thirty-two years cannot afford the new rent.
Who has sold dried chillies the trader from the same stall for thirty-two years cannot afford the new rent.
Cannot afford the new rent the trader who has sold dried chillies for thirty-two years from the same stall.
From the same stall for thirty-two years the trader who has sold dried chillies cannot afford the new rent.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'who has sold dried chillies from the same stall for thirty-two years' is a defining relative clause specifying exactly which trader the essay means.
mcQuestions.hint: Defining relative clauses follow the noun directly and start with 'who', 'that', or 'which'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'to be flattened into a brand' mean for a market?
options: To be physically flattened by demolition
To lose its texture, irregularity, and local character so that it becomes a uniform, marketable image
To be reduced in price across all stalls at once
To be permanently closed by local authorities
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Flattened into a brand' is a metaphor for the loss of complexity and unevenness as a place is smoothed into a single image that can be marketed.
mcQuestions.hint: What does 'flatten' do to a complex shape, and what is a 'brand' — and what happens when one becomes the other?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best closes the essay by defining what is being defended?
options: In conclusion, markets are important to many cities.
Therefore, all food halls must be demolished.

What needs defending here is not nostalgia for dirt. It is the lived-in market — the building where neighbourhood provisioning, intergenerational stallholding, and a particular form of working-class commerce can still take place — and the recognition that this kind of room, once flattened into a brand, does not return.
To sum up, both old markets and new food halls have their advantages.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C names what is being defended precisely (the lived-in market), distinguishes it from a strawman (nostalgia for dirt), and ends with the irreversibility of the change.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option states exactly what is being defended and what is not?

mcQuestions.prompt: What example does the essay cite as evidence that good renovation is possible?
options: Spanish municipalities that have funded structural upgrades while keeping rent ceilings and tenancy protections
London markets that have replaced all incumbents with restaurants
A specific German shopping centre converted from a chemical plant
A demolished market in Paris that was rebuilt as housing
mcQuestions.explanation: Paragraph five names Spanish municipalities specifically as evidence that the choice between a working market and a clean building is, in many cases, false.
mcQuestions.hint: Look at paragraph five for the writer's named example.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a market, shop street, or other commercial space near you that has been or could be 'modernised'. Walk through it (in your mind or in person) and list the people whose presence would not survive the renovation. What would you lose along with them, and what — if anything — would make it worth the trade?
